coredata.dc:title: A new type of cationic surfactant prepared by surface modification on sucrose carbon dots via an entirely green approach and the inhibition on growth of E. coli and S. aureus
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jian
Liu, Xiang
coredata.dc:description: Sucrose was renewable resource which was a raw material for preparing carbon dots (CDs) since it was readily to be carbonized. Chemical modification could realize a tremendous change of the performance of the CDs. A novel technique of preparing a new type of cationic surfactant derived from sucrose CDs (SCDs) was described herein in detail. Firstly, SCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal treatment of 180° on the mixture of 1.0 g aqueous sucrose solution and 200 μL hydrogen peroxide for 2.0 h. Then SCDs were modified chemically by virtue of reflux heating on mixture of SCDs and n-dodecylamine in aqueous medium for realizing chemical bonding between hydrophobic hydrocarbon chain in n-dodecylamine and SCDs (represented with SCDs-C12). SCDs-C12 declined surface tension of water from 72.0 to 32.9 mN m−1 which was comparable to several traditional surfactants or novel cationic surfactants. Moreover, any organic solvent was never used in the whole procedure. Zeta potential measurement indicated SCDs-C12 was positive charged which declared SCDs-C12 belonged to cationic surfactant. Antibiosis experiments demonstrated SCDs-C12 performed wonderfully in inhibiting the growth of E. coli and S. aureus. Thus we provided a green chemical approach of preparing a new type of cationic surfactant with inexpensive raw material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sucrose
Carbon dots
Cationic surfactant
Surface tension
Antibiosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of SCDs and chemical modification on SCDs with n-dodecylamine
Measurements on surface tension and contact angle of SCDs-C12 solutions
Antibiosis of SCDs and SCDs-C12 solutions on E. coli and S. aureus
Antibiosis of SCDs-C12 on E. coli and S. aureus

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description about preparation of SCDs-C12 surfactant. SCDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment on sucrose solution in the present of hydrogen peroxide. SCDs-C12 surfactants were produced by reflux heating on mixture of SCDs and n-dodecylamine in aqueous medium. The surface activity and antibiosis for E. coli and S. aureus of SCDs-C12 were investigated in detail.
Images of transmission electron microscopy of SCDs and SCDs-C12 corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively. The particle size distributions in (a) and (b) are indicated in (c) and (d) respectively.
IR spectra of SCDs and SCDs-C12.
C 1s spectra of SCDs and SCDs-C12 corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively; (c) Range of 289.8–285.0 eV in (b) is magnified for a clear observation; (d) N 1s spectrum of SCDs-C12.

(a) Surface tensions of solution of the products prepared by modifications on 0.5 g SCDs with 0.05, 0.10, 0.15 g n-dodecylamine respectively. (b) Plot of the surface tensions of SCDs-C12 solutions varied with natural logarithm of the concentration (ln(c)).
Foaming and foam stability of SCDs-C12 compared with APG 1200, SDBS and DTAC within 60 min (all the concentrations are 5.0 mg mL−1).
(a) Zeta potentials of SCDs and SCDs-C12 and postulations on the surface charge corresponding to (b) and (c) respectively.
(a, b) Inhibition rates of SCDs and SCDs-C12 on E. coli or S. aureus; (c, d) Photographs of colony-forming of E. coli or S. aureus in the presence of SCDs and SCDs-C12.
Percentages of the peak areas in Fig. 4.
Performances of several surfactants described in references.

row.entry: Triethanolamine, bromoacetyl bromide, dimethyl amine derivative [28]
Sucrose, H2O2, n-dodecylamine; Glucose, H2O2, n-dodecylamine (This work)

head.ce:title: A new type of cationic surfactant prepared by surface modification on sucrose carbon dots via an entirely green approach and the inhibition on growth of E. coli and S. aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, 243002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sucrose was renewable resource which was a raw material for preparing carbon dots (CDs) since it was readily to be carbonized. Chemical modification could realize a tremendous change of the performance of the CDs. A novel technique of preparing a new type of cationic surfactant derived from sucrose CDs (SCDs) was described herein in detail. Firstly, SCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal treatment of 180° on the mixture of 1.0 g aqueous sucrose solution and 200 μL hydrogen peroxide for 2.0 h. Then SCDs were modified chemically by virtue of reflux heating on mixture of SCDs and n-dodecylamine in aqueous medium for realizing chemical bonding between hydrophobic hydrocarbon chain in n-dodecylamine and SCDs (represented with SCDs-C12). SCDs-C12 declined surface tension of water from 72.0 to 32.9 mN m−1 which was comparable to several traditional surfactants or novel cationic surfactants. Moreover, any organic solvent was never used in the whole procedure. Zeta potential measurement indicated SCDs-C12 was positive charged which declared SCDs-C12 belonged to cationic surfactant. Antibiosis experiments demonstrated SCDs-C12 performed wonderfully in inhibiting the growth of E. coli and S. aureus. Thus we provided a green chemical approach of preparing a new type of cationic surfactant with inexpensive raw material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sucrose is a renewable resource that is applied extensive in food manufacturing, which provides energy for human being. Also it is a raw material for manufacturing sucrose ester which is a nonionic surfactant that applied as food or cosmetic additive due to the wonderful emulsification properties and weak toxicity [1–5]. Additionally, sucrose ester was applied in pharmaceutical area which acted as a natural and biodegradable excipient depending on the perfect emulsifying and solubilizing behavior [6]. Also, it was a main component of contact lens care solution [7]. The hydroxyl groups in sucrose could not only be esterified by higher fatty acid (the product was sucrose ester) but also be involved in the addition reaction with 1,2-epoxydodecane in the presence of tertiary amine [8]. The hydrophobicity of the attached hydrocarbon chain and the inherent hydrophilic groups of sucrose created the hydrophile-lipophile balance in aqueous medium, which endowed the surface activity. Apart from these, sucrose was a common starting material for making carbon nano dots (CDs) in aqueous medium [9–11] depending on the wonderful solubility and higher ratio of carbon element. Violent treatments such as hydrothermal or microwave heating usually leaded to break of the covalent bond accompanied with complicate chemical reactions that resulted in production of CDs. However, the CDs emitted blue fluorescence usually with lower fluorescence quantum yield that restricted the application due to the shorter emission and the weak fluorescence. For instance, the CDs made by hydrothermal disposing on sucrose solution owned blue emission with 4 % fluorescence quantum yield (400 nm excitation) [10]. Microwave treatment on sucrose solution in the present of several inorganic ions accelerated production of the CDs. The emission was less than 500 nm with less than 10 % fluorescence quantum yield [9]. Such a photoluminescence performance manifested they were almost impossible to be practical applied. In fact, sucrose was applied predominantly in food manufacturing. It was difficult to transform sucrose into other valuable chemicals since it was short of more reactive groups including the weak solubility in organic solvents. Preparation of the CDs might provide a route of utilization of sucrose since chemical reactivity of the CDs was enhanced compared with that of sucrose due to the new occurred functional groups on the surface such as carbonyl, aldehyde, carboxyl groups etc. when aqueous sucrose solution was suffered from violent treatments [12,13].

Herein, we provide a novel route of making a new type of surfactant derived from sucrose. The sucrose solution was disposed under hydrothermal treatment in the presence of hydrogen peroxide for preparing the carbon nano dots (represented with SCDs). Then chemical modification with n-dodecylamine was executed for obtaining the surfactant (represented with SCDs-C12), which was apt to foam in aqueous medium and was effective on inhibiting growth of E. coli and S. aureus. The technique was described schematically in Fig. 1 .

Sucrose (analytical reagent), n-octylamine (99 %), n-decylamine (98 %), n-dodecylamine (98 %), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS, 95 %) and dodecyltrimethylammonium chloride (DTAC, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai China). APG (Alkyl poly(glycoside)) 1200 was obtained from BASF Company. Hydrogen peroxide (30 wt %) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Double distilled water was used all though the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SCDs and chemical modification on SCDs with n-dodecylamine
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 1.0 g sucrose and dissolved in 10 mL water. The solution was mixed with 200 μL hydrogen peroxide (30 wt %). Then the mixed solution was put into a Teflon reactor with stainless shells for a hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 60 min. After a period cooling at room temperature the yellow solution in the reactor was treated with reduced-pressure distillation for obtaining SCDs. Finally, SCDs solution (100 mg mL−1) was prepared for successive chemical modification.

Typically, weighted 0.10 g n-dodecylamine and mixed with 10 mL water in a beaker. SCDs solution of 5 mL (100 mg mL−1) was added into the mixture. It was transferred into a round flask with a reflux heating at 80 °C for 2 h for obtaining dodecyl-modified SCDs (represented with SCDs-C12). Similarly, 0.05 and 0.15 g n-dodecylamine were weighed to execute the similar experiment respectively for investigating effect of the amount of n-dodecylamine on capability of the product in declining surface tension of water.

Similarly, aqueous glucose solution (1.0 g in 10 mL water) mixed with 200 μL hydrogen peroxide were suffered from hydrothermal treatment under 180 °C for 60 min for preparing glucose CDs (represented with GCDs). The chemical modification on GCDs solution (5 mL, 100 mg mL−1) with n-dodecylamine (0.10 g) was carried out for attaching the dodecyl onto GCDs chemically (represented with GCDs-C12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements on surface tension and contact angle of SCDs-C12 solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Surface tensions of SCDs-C12 solutions (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 15 mg mL−1) were acquired by comparing the additional pressure of bubbles of the solutions with that of pure water (the surface tension was 72.0 mN m−1 at 25 °C) according to Laplace equation. Contact angle of SCDs-C12 solution was obtained by photographing the drop of SCDs-C12 solution on a clean silicon chip. This also provided an evidence of demonstrating the surface activity of SCDs-C12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiosis of SCDs and SCDs-C12 solutions on E. coli and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibition on growth of E. coli and S. aureus was investigated by comparing the optical density at 600 nm wavelength of the culturing mixture in the present of SCDs or SCDs-C12 solutions (0.4, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, 0.025 mg mL−1) with the control, which described in our previous work [14]. Colony-forming assays also provided evidences of demonstrating the antibiosis of SCDs or SCDs-C12. Thus minimal inhibition concentrations (MIC) of SCDs-C12 for E. coli and S. aureus were then achieved by analyses on the histograms and the photographs of colony-forming.

The reactivity of SCDs to n-dodecylamine was demonstrated by analyses on variations of infrared (IR) and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS). The aqueous solutions of SCDs or SCDs-C12 (5 μL) were dropped on a KBr pellet (dried under a heat lamp) for executing IR measurement. C 1s and N 1s signals of XPS were analyzed in detail via multi-peak fitting with different ratio of Gaussian or Lorentz functions for obtaining variations of percentages of the surface groups of SCDs and SCDs-C12. These provided solid evidences of demonstrating the chemical bonding of the alkyl chain in n-dodecylamine with SCDs and proving success of the modification. SCDs or SCDs-C12 solution (800 μL) of 5 mg mL−1 were put into a cuvette with Y-type and the zeta potential was measured with Laser Particle Size and Zeta Potential Analyzer (Nano ZS90c). This announced which type of surfactant that SCDs-C12 belonged.

Sucrose, as a renewable resource, is one of the most common carbohydrates. It is applied extensively in food manufacturing. In fact, sucrose is rarely to be as a resource applied in chemical industry due to the complete insolubility in organic solvent. Several reports describe that some carbon nano dots (CDs) are made from hydrothermal treatment on sucrose solution. However, it emits blue fluorescence that restricts the application in fluorescent imaging. The intensive disposing on sucrose brings about the carbonization accompanied with oxidization of the resource. Fig. 2 (c) reveals that the most particle sizes of SCDs in Fig. 2(a) are distributed in rang of 1.35–1.95 nm. Fig. 2(d) indicates the size of SCDs-C12 in Fig. 2(b) is in range of 2.05–2.95 nm. Reflux heating on mixture of SCDs and n-dodecylamine under 80 °C causes the increase of the particle size. The size enhancement predicts that the long hydrocarbon chain of n-dodecylamine is attached onto surface of SCDs, which should be attributed to the combination between SCDs and n-dodecylamine. Both SCDs and SCDs-C12 emit blue fluorescence (Fig. S1 of Supporting Information), which are alike to most CDs made from carbohydrates [9,10,15–18]. In fact, the similar treatment on mixture of GCDs and n-dodecylamine also causes the increase of the particle size, which exhibited in Fig. S2 of Supporting Information. This also assigns to the chemical bonding of alkyl in n-dodecylamine with GCDs.

IR spectra of Fig. 3 reveal the structural change when mixture of SCDs and n-dodecylamine is heated at 80 °C for 2 h. The peaks at 1773 and 1731 cm−1 of SCDs are due to stretching vibration of CO (v(CO)) of ester and carboxyl group [9] respectively. Band of 1640 cm−1 is due to the v(CC) in SCDs [19]. This declares that the hydrothermal treatment leads to esterification among the decomposed pieces of sucrose. Some carboxyl groups on the decomposed pieces remain intact during the esterification. Most importantly, it forecasts the production of carboxyl on SCDs surface, which comes from the violent oxidization of sucrose [12,20]. The intensive peak at 1040 cm−1 is attributed to symmetrical vibration of C–O–C of ether [19] in SCDs. The wide peak centered at 3400 cm−1 is due to vibration of O–H of hydroxyl. The hydroxyl and the ether groups ensure the hydrophilicity of SCDs. Chemical modification with n-dodecylamine does not eliminate such hydrophilic groups. However, it depresses the transmittance of v(CO). Additionally, the obvious alkyl signal is observed from the spectrum of SCDs-C12. Peaks of 2923 and 2854 cm−1 are assigned to asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibration of C–H of methylene. The weak peak at 2952 cm−1 belongs to v(C–H) of methyl, which comes from the terminal of hydrocarbon chain in n-dodecylamine. Such signals predict the combination between SCDs and n-dodecylamine. SCDs-C12 particles are produced by the reaction between primary amino groups of n-dodecylamine and the carboxyl or ester groups of SCDs. The peak centered at 1603 cm−1 should be overlapping of v(CC) and v(CO) of amide I. The spectrum of SCDs-C12 also exhibits that the hydrophilicity such as ether and hydroxyl groups in SCDs-C12 are intact. The core of SCDs does not change during the chemical modification.

XPS of Fig. 4 also demonstrates the combination between SCDs and n-dodecylamine. C 1s signal of Fig. 4(a) exhibits that there are four characteristic peaks, obtained by multi-peak fitting operation. The peaks of 284.4, 285.8, 287.1 and 288.6 eV correspond to signals of C–C/C C [21], C–O [21], C O and C(=O)–O [22,23] species respectively. The ratio of C–O arrives at 53.7 %, listed in Table 1 . This exclaims that the ether or hydroxyl is predominant in surface of SCDs, which accords with IR spectra analyses on SCDs. It contributes the well hydrophilicity of SCDs. The ratio of C–C/C C is 31.0 %, which announces the carbonization of sucrose under hydrothermal treatment (such ratio in sucrose is 3.5 % theoretically). The surface C O and C(=O)–O are derived from oxidization of sucrose. That indicates aldehyde [12] or carbonyl on surface of SCDs. C(=O)–O derived from carboxyl or ester accounts to 2.4 %. Fig. 4(b) exhibits there are still four components on surface of SCDs-C12, which corresponding to peaks of 284.3, 285.7, 287.2 and 288.4 eV. The weak peak, enlarged in Fig. 4(c), at 288.4 eV is also assigned to C(=O)–O. The ratio of the peak area in Fig. 4(a) declines to 0.7 % in Fig. 4(b) while the ratio of C–C/C C in Fig. 4(b) increases to 37.5 % (listed in Table 1). This announces that the surface carboxyl or ester of SCDs is consumed and alkyl of n-dodecylamine is attached chemically to SCDs. It reveals that the surface carboxyl is the key reaction site for the modification via amidation between n-dodecylamine and SCDs. The N 1s spectrum of SCDs-C12 (Fig. 4(d)) indicates clearly there are two components at 399.2 and 401.1 eV which is due to NHCO and NH2 +CO. Protonation of the part amide causes shift of the binding energy.

The modification with n-dodecylamine is successful which demonstrated by the aforementioned analyses on the spectra. Surface hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, ether or carbonyl ensure the hydrophilicity of SCDs. The attached n-dodecyl endows the hydrophobicity. Presumably, such an amphipathic SCDs-C12 has the capability of declining surface tension of water according to the principle of surface activity. The modification with 0.05, 0.10 or 0.15 g n-dodecylamine on 0.5 g SCDs bring about the amphipathy of the product, which witnessed by Fig. 5(a ∼ c). However, the modification with of 0.15 g n-dodecylamine (Fig. 5(b)) leads to the least surface tension of the solution (32.4 mN m−1) at the critical micelle concentration of 10 mg mL−1 compared with that of 0.05 or 0.10 g dosage (corresponding to Fig. 5(a) and (c)). Experiments reveal that more amount of n-dodecylamine is excess for the chemical modification. During the experiment of reflux heating on mixture of SCDS and n-dodecylamine, the excess (oily liquid) floats in the surface of SCDs solution permanently that forecasts n-dodecylamine is overdosed. Fig. 5(b) exhibit the surface tension of water is declined rapidly as the increase of the concentration, which acts as a traditional surfactant completely.

Decrease of the contact angles of SCDs-C12 solution with the increased concentrations also proves the surface activity, which indicated in Fig. S2 of Supporting Information. This agrees with the curve (0.15 g dedecylamine) of Fig. 5 (a). The decline of surface tension of SCDs-C12 solution with the increase of the concentration agrees with Langmuir absorption model approximately, which described with formula of (1).
(1)
Γ
Γ m
=
k · c
1 + k · c
where c and Γ presents concentration of SCDs-C12 and surface absorption capacity respectively; k and Γm (saturated absorption capacity) are constants. When k·c is far less than 1 (in extremely dilute solution of SCDs-C12) the Γ is proportional to the concentration of c. The surface tension declines proportionally with the concentration. When k·c is far greater than 1 (c is greater than the critical micelle concentration) the Γ approach Γm. The surface tension reaches a minimum.

According to the principle of surface activity, all kinds of surfactants are apt to foam. Foaming and foam stability are the characteristics of a surfactant. Such performances (25 °C) of SCDs-C12 are demonstrated by Fig. 6 . It excels traditional surfactants such as APG 1200, SDBS and DTAC (they are typical nonionic, anionic and cationic surfactants) since the foam volume of SCDs-C12 solution is the largest within 60 min. The foam volume is twice more as much of SDBS. This proves the wonderful surface activity of SCDs-C12.

Fig. 5(b) declares that the variation of surface tension of SCDs-C12 solution with ln(c). The surface tension is nearly linear with ln(c) when the concentrations are less than 10 mg mL−1. This satisfies Gibbs absorption isotherm equation in a defined temperature which described in formula (2).
(2)
Γ = −
1
n · R T
d γ
d
ln
c
where R is gaseous constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1); T is temperature (K); γ represents surface tension; n is the number of ionic species at solution/air interface including counter ions [24]. Slope of the fitted line in Fig. 5(d) is −12.78 mN m−1. Herein, we assume n = 3. When concentration of SCDs-C12 solution approaches 10 mg mL−1 the surface absorption capacity Γ arrives a maximum, which is 1.718 × 10−6 mol m−2 calculated according to formula (2). The sectional area (Am) of the hydrophobic chain in SCDs-C12 can be calculated according to formula (3).
(3)
A m
=
10 18
L ·
Γ max
where L is Avogadro constant (6.022 × 1023 mol−1). So Am = 0.966 nm2. This approaches the saturated absorption capacity of a gemini surfactants reported in the reference [25] (n = 3), in which the length of the hydrophobic chain is the same as the one of SCDs-C12. That suggests two hydrophobic chains are attached with SCDs and SCDs-C12 belongs to a new type of gemini surfactant.

Zeta potential measurements on SCDs and SCDs-C12 reveal that SCDs is negative charged while SCDs-C12 is positive charged, which exhibited in Fig. 7 (a). It is interested that the convenient chemical modification on SCDs alerts the tremendous variation of the surface charge. The negative charge on SCDs is due to the ionization of the surface carboxyl groups, displayed in Fig. 7(b). The surface amide of SCDs-C12 couples with proton that leads to the surface positive charge accompanied with the production of OH− drawn in Fig. 7(c). Such an explanation is supported by N 1s spectra of Fig. 4(d), in which the split peaks are assigned to protonation of the amide.

According to the surface potential we conclude that SCDs-C12 belongs to a cationic gemini surfactant. For almost all the conventional cation surfactant, the positive charges are due to the quaternary ammonium in a surfactant. For SCDs-C12, the positive charge has nothing to do with quaternary ammonium that differs with conventional cation surfactant. For a more clear understanding on the surface activity of SCDs-C12, such a performance of several gemini surfactants are summarized in Table 2 .

Obviously, the performance of surface activity of SCDs-C12 is comparable to several novel gemini surfactants by comparing the least surface tension in aqueous medium. However, the cost of synthesizing these surfactants is higher than that of SCDs-C12 because of the expensive raw materials. Most importantly, the syntheses must be carried out in organic solvent which listed in Table 2 while SCDs-C12 is obtained by hydrothermal and successive amidation in aqueous medium. From the view of this point, the technique of preparing SCDs-C12 satisfies the demands of green production.

Additionally, when mixture of n-octylamine or n-decylamine and SCDs solution is heated at 80 °C large amounts of insoluble substances occur in the reaction vessel. This suggests that more octyls or decyls are attached chemically onto SCDs that enhances hydrophobicity of the product seriously. Obviously, the reactivity of n-octylamine or n-decylamine is higher than that of n-dodecylamine because of the shorter hydrocarbon chain. However, the more bonded hydrocarbon chains destroy hydrophile-lipophile balance of the product. The proper density of dodecyl on SCDs guarantees the high surface activity of SCDS-C12. Summarily, the surface carboxyl of SCDs is the key reactive site for the successive amidation, as is analyzed on XPS of Fig. 4(a) and (b). It comes from the violent oxidization under hydrothermal condition in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. The reactivity of surface carboxyl is also described in our previous report [32], in which the chemical modification on the CDs with m-phenylenediamine arouses distinct red-shift of fluorescent emission.

Similarly, glucose can also be a candidate of making glucose surfactant. Hydrothermal treatment on glucose solution induces the formation of GCDs (demonstrated in Fig. S3(a)). Chemical modification on GCDs with n-dodecylamine enhances the particle size compared with that of GCDs (exhibited in Fig. S3(b)). The performance of declining surface tension of water is proved by Fig. S4 in Supporting Information. The least surface tension of water in the presence of GCDs-C12 arrives 30.8 mN m−1, which is also comparable to some gemini surfactants.

The chemical modification also gives rise to tremendous change of the zeta potential of GCDs, shown in Fig. S5. GCDs-C12 is still positive charged due to the coupling of surface amide with proton. It also belongs to cationic surfactant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiosis of SCDs-C12 on E. coli and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: The antibiosis of SCDs-C12 is revealed by Fig. 8 . Fig. 8(a) and (b) exhibited that SCDs-C12 is effective on inhibiting the growth of E. coli and S. aureus. The inhibition rate for E. coli reaches 97.2 % when concentration of SCDs-C12 is 0.2 mg mL−1 (MIC). When the concentration is 0.4 mg mL−1 (MIC) the inhibition rate for S. aureus arrives 100 %. However, SCDs do not have any effect for E. coli or S. aureus. They are even nutritious substances for growth of E. coli. The photographs of colony-forming also support the analyses on Fig. 8(a) and (b). In the presence of SCDs solution, E. coli and S. aureus are cultivated well. When the concentration of SCDs-C12 is greater than 0.2 mg mL−1 the growth of E. coli is inhibited completely within 18 h. For S. aureus the effective concentration is greater than 0.4 mg mL−1 within 18 h.

That is an interesting performance of SCDs-C12. The convenient chemical modification with n-dodecylamine brings about not only the wonderful surface activity in reducing surface tension of water but also the enhancement of the antibiosis. Such a performance is analogous to several new types of cationic surfactant [33,34] whose solutions took also effect on inhibiting the growth of E. coli or S. aureus. That endows the versatility of SCDs-C12.

We develop a novel route of making a cationic surfactant with sucrose, which differs entirely with the traditional one in chemical structure. The inexpensive sucrose or glucose, as a renewable resource, provides abundant raw material for producing SCDs or GCDs. The surface groups provide a natural hydrophilicity. A convenient modification with n-dodecylamine realizes the chemical bonding of the long hydrocarbon chain with SCDs or GCDs. That increases the hydrophobicity of SCDs or GCDs. Such a hydrophile-lipophile balance endours the surface activity of SCDs-C12 or GCDs-C12. Most importantly, none of organic solvents is used throughout the work. That satisfies the demands of green chemical industry.
Jian Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carres.2025.109541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface activity of monoesters fatty acid esters of sucrose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L Osipow, F D Snell, D Marra, W C York. Surface activity of monoesters fatty acid esters of sucrose. Ind. Eng. Chem. 48 (1956) 1462-1464.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mixed surfactant systems of sucrose esters and lecithin as a synergistic approach for oil structuring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M D Bin Sintang, S Danthine, A R Patel, T Rimaux, D Van De Walle, K Dewettinck. Mixed surfactant systems of sucrose esters and lecithin as a synergistic approach for oil structuring. J. Colloid Interface Sci. 504 (2017) 387-396. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.05.114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Edible oil-water foamulsions stabilized by vesicle network of sucrose ester

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R Zheng, X Hu, C Su, J Jiang, Z Cui, B P Binks. Edible oil-water foamulsions stabilized by vesicle network of sucrose ester. J. Mol. Liq. 371 (2023) 121066. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.121066.
